text.content:
• Catatonia is not a discrete psychiatric condition but a phenomenon that can be seen in oth mood and psychotic disorders
  • Per DSM-V, three of the following needed to diagnose catatonia: stupor, catalepsy, waxy flexibility, mutism, negativism, posturing, mannerisms, stereotypy, agitation, grimacing, echolalia, and echopraxia
Catatonia per DSM-V
SignDescription
StuporNo psychomotor activity; the person is unresponsive and does not interact with the environment.
CatalepsyPerson maintains a posture placed by another, even against gravity.
Waxy FlexibilitySlight resistance to movement when positioned by an examiner, as if the limbs were made of wax.
MutismVery limited or complete absence of verbal response (not due to language impairment like aphasia).
NegativismResistance to instructions or external stimuli, or no response at all, without any apparent reason.
PosturingActively holding a rigid or unusual posture against gravity without external prompting.
MannerismOdd or exaggerated performance of normal actions, often appearing theatrical or bizarre.
StereotypyRepetitive, purposeless movements performed frequently (e.g., rocking, hand-flapping).
AgitationExcessive motor activity that is not caused or influenced by the surroundings.
GrimacingRepetitive or inappropriate facial expressions, such as twisted or exaggerated movements.
EcholaliaImitation or repetition of another person’s spoken words.
EchopraxiaImitation of another person’s movements or gestures.
N.B.Bush-Francis scale also includes signs of automatic obedience, autonomic abnormalities and grasp reflex as indicative of catatonia.
 

Catatonic stupor, with features of catalepsy
Public domain

text.content:
CategoryKey Points
DefinitionBenign, common 'stuck-on' warty or waxy skin growth seen with aging; not malignant.
Epidemiology & CausesVery common in adults (esp. > 60 yrs); cause unknown; related to aging, friction, sun exposure, and genetic mutations.
Clinical FeaturesVariable size/colour; flat or raised papules/plaques with waxy surface; can be solitary or grouped; palms/soles spared.
DiagnosisClinical appearance ('stuck-on' plaque); dermoscopy or biopsy when uncertain (needs to exclude skin cancer).
Management & PrognosisNo treatment needed unless bothersome; removal options include cryotherapy, curettage/electrocautery, laser, shave; lesions persist and may recur.

text.content:
CategoryKey Points
Definition & TypesEczematous reaction from skin contact with external substances. Main types: Irritant (direct barrier damage) and Allergic (type IV delayed hypersensitivity)
Risk & CausesMost common occupational skin disease; high risk = wet work (healthcare, cleaners, hairdressers, food handlers). Triggers: soaps, detergents, chemicals, metals, cosmetics
Clinical FeaturesItchy erythematous rash ± vesicles, swelling. Chronic disease → dryness, fissures, scaling, lichenification. Usually limited to contact site; allergic type may spread
DiagnosisClinical: rash pattern + exposure history. Patch testing confirms allergic contact dermatitis
ManagementAvoid trigger (most important), emollients/barrier care, topical corticosteroids. Severe cases → systemic steroids or immunosuppressive therapy

text.content:
CategoryKey Points
DefinitionChronic pruritic immunobullous skin disease that is the cutaneous manifestation of coeliac disease (gluten-sensitive enteropathy); not related to herpes despite the name.
Pathophysiology / AssociationsGluten intolerance (wheat, rye, barley) → IgA autoantibodies → immune deposition in skin and gut; >90% have underlying gluten-sensitive enteropathy; associated with HLA-DQ2/DQ8 and other autoimmune diseases. 
Clinical FeaturesIntensely itchy symmetrical vesicles/papules (often excoriated) on erythematous skin; classic distribution: elbows, knees, buttocks, scalp, shoulders; lesions heal with pigment change.
DiagnosisSkin biopsy + direct immunofluorescence of perilesional skin showing granular IgA deposition at dermal papillae (gold standard); coeliac serology (e.g., anti-endomysial, tissue transglutaminase antibodies).
Management & PrognosisLifelong gluten-free diet (key treatment); dapsone provides rapid symptomatic relief; alternatives include steroids or immunosuppressants if intolerant; prognosis good with treatment.

rich_text.plain_text:
CategoryKey Points

Pathophysiology / AssociationsGluten intolerance (wheat, rye, barley) → IgA autoantibodies → immune deposition in skin and gut; >90% have underlying gluten-sensitive enteropathy; associated with HLA-DQ2/DQ8 and other autoimmune diseases. 
Clinical FeaturesIntensely itchy symmetrical vesicles/papules (often excoriated) on erythematous skin; classic distribution: elbows, knees, buttocks, scalp, shoulders; lesions heal with pigment change.
DiagnosisSkin biopsy + direct immunofluorescence of perilesional skin showing granular IgA deposition at dermal papillae (gold standard); coeliac serology (e.g., anti-endomysial, tissue transglutaminase antibodies).
Management & PrognosisLifelong gluten-free diet (key treatment); dapsone provides rapid symptomatic relief; alternatives include steroids or immunosuppressants if intolerant; prognosis good with treatment.

text.content:
CategoryKey Points
DefinitionCommon keratinocyte skin cancer arising from epidermal keratinocytes; invasive and can metastasise (regional lymph nodes most commonly).
Risk Factors / CausesChronic UV exposure (UVB) is the main cause; associated with actinic keratoses, fair skin, older age, smoking, chronic wounds/scars, immunosuppression (e.g., transplant), arsenic/radiation exposure, and certain genetic syndromes.
Clinical FeaturesEnlarging scaly or crusted nodule/plaque that may ulcerate and be tender; develops over weeks–months, often arising from actinic keratosis; occurs mainly on sun-exposed sites (face, ears, lips, hands, forearms, legs).
Diagnosis & Risk StratificationUsually clinical → confirmed with biopsy. High-risk features include ≥2 cm size, depth >2 mm, poor differentiation, perineural/vascular invasion, high-risk sites (ear/lip/face), immunosuppression.
Management & PrognosisSurgical excision (3–10 mm margins) is standard; alternatives: curettage/electrocautery, cryotherapy (low-risk), Mohs surgery, or radiotherapy. Advanced disease may need systemic therapy (e.g., cemiplimab). Most are curable if treated early.

Management & PrognosisSurgical excision (3–10 mm margins) is standard; alternatives: curettage/electrocautery, cryotherapy (low-risk), Mohs surgery, or radiotherapy. Advanced disease may need systemic therapy (e.g., cemiplimab). Most are curable if treated early.

pages._block_html:
DSM-V Depression TypeClinical Features
UnspecifiedDoesn’t fully meet criteria; vague or insufficient information
With Anxious DistressProminent anxiety symptoms during depression
With Mixed FeaturesSome manic/hypomanic symptoms present during depression
With Melancholic FeaturesLoss of pleasure + distinct quality of mood + psychomotor agitation or slowing
With Atypical FeaturesMood brightens with positive events + reversed vegetative symptoms (e.g. increased sleep/appetite)
With Psychotic FeaturesPresence of delusions or hallucinations
With CatatoniaSignificant abnormal movement or behavioural changes during episode
With Peripartum OnsetDepression during pregnancy or soon after childbirth
With Seasonal PatternDepression linked to specific seasons, recurring regularly

text.content:
Neuroprotective measures in traumatic brain injury
TierInterventionKey points
First30° head upImproves venous drainage
Sedation / analgesia
Neuromuscular blockadeControls ICP; increases pneumonia risk
CSF drainage (EVD)If ICP elevated
Mild hyperventilationTarget PaCO₂ 35 mmHg
SecondMannitol0.25 - 1 g/kg every 3 hours
Hypertonic saline3%: 3 mL/kg or 20%: 10 - 20 mL
FinalBarbiturate comaLowers CMRO₂; hypotension risk
HypothermiaLowers ICP; no proven outcome benefit
Aggressive hyperventilationRescue only (herniation)
Decompressive craniectomy
Lumbar drainage
OtherAvoid hyperthermiaCool aggressively if > 39°C
Seizure prophylaxisPhenytoin or levetiracetam × 7 days
ICP: intracranial pressure
EVD: external ventricular drain
CMRO₂: cerebral metabolic rate of oxygen

text.content:
Shock
Feature Hypovolemic Cardiogenic Obstructive Distributive
Cause Blood/fluid loss Pump failure (e.g. MI, HF) PE, tamponade, tension pneumothorax Sepsis, anaphylaxis, CNS injury
Pathophysiology ↓ preload ↓ contractility ↓ cardiac filling/outflow ↓ SVR (vasodilation)
Clinical features Cold skin, weak pulses, low urine output Cold skin, elevated JVP, pulmonary crackles, signs of chest trauma Distended neck veins, muffled heart sounds, absent breath sounds (pneumothorax), possible chest trauma Warm, flushed skin, low diastolic pressure, signs of spinal injury/infection/allergy
Diagnosis Clinical + hemodynamics ECG, echo Imaging (CT, echo) Clinical +/- cultures
Management Fluids, blood Inotropes Relieve obstruction Fluids, vasopressors, treat cause

text.content:
DSM-5 Major Depressive Disorder
Criterion Simplified Description
A ≥ 5 symptoms in same 2-week period, with change in functioning.
One must be low mood or loss of interest.
  • Low mood most of the day, nearly every day (can be irritable in youth)
  • Loss of interest or pleasure in most activities
  • Weight/appetite change (≥ 5% body weight/month)
  • Insomnia or hypersomnia
  • Psychomotor agitation or retardation (observable by others)
  • Fatigue or low energy
  • Feelings of worthlessness or excessive guilt
  • Diminished ability to think or concentrate
  • Recurrent thoughts of death or suicide
B Symptoms cause significant distress or impairment in social, occupational, or other areas of functioning.
C Not attributable to the physiological effects of a substance or another medical condition.
D Not better explained by schizoaffective disorder, schizophrenia, or other psychotic disorders.
E No history of manic or hypomanic episodes (unless substance/medical-related).
Specifiers With anxious distress, mixed features, melancholic features, atypical features, psychotic features, catatonia, peripartum onset, seasonal pattern.
Based on Diagnostic and Statistical Manual of Mental Disorders, Fifth Edition, 2013. American Psychiatric Association.

text.content:
• Buruli ulcers are caused by infection by Mycobacterium ulcerans
  • Buruli ulcers are also known as Bairnsdale or Daintree ulcer
  • It can also present as an atypical cellulitis, or as a plaque

Reproduced with permission from The Royal Australian College of General Practitioners from: From Wong CCW, Muhi S, O'Brien DP. An overview of Buruli ulcer in Australia. Aust J Gen Pract. 2024;53(9):671-674. Available at:

text.content:
• Erythrasma is characterised by flexural rashes with brown or pink, well-demarcated patches and plaques, with fine scaling and superficial fissures
  • Caused by overgrowth of the commensal bacterium C. minutissimum

Left: erythrasma. Right: Wood light fluroescence.

text.content:
Fitzpatrick skin phototypes
Type
Characteristic appearance
Response to sun exposure
MED (mJ/cm²)
I
Very light skin, often with blue or hazel eyes and fair or red hair
Consistently burns, does not develop a tan
15–30
II
Light complexion, frequently blue eyes
Burns readily, minimal tanning
25–40
III
Medium/light brown skin tone
May burn initially, then gradually tans
30–50
IV
Olive or light brown skin
Rarely burns, tans with ease
40–60
V
Dark brown skin
Very uncommon to burn, tans deeply
60–90
VI 
Deeply pigmented dark brown to black skin
Does not burn, always develops a dark tan
90–150
  • MED: minimal erythema dose
  • Table adapted from

text.content:
• Ramsay-Hunt is caused by reactivation of varicella zoster virus (VSV) in geniculate ganglion of the Facial Nerve (CN VII)
  • Ramsay-Hunt syndrome is also known as herpes zoster oticus (not to be confused with herpes zoster ophthalmicus)

PaO₂< 7.9 kPa1
Age> 55 years1
Neutrophils (WBC)> 15 × 10⁹/L1
Calcium< 2 mmol/L1
Renal function (Urea)> 16 mmol/L1

Albumin (serum)< 32 g/L1
Sugar (Blood glucose)> 10 mmol/L1
Maximum Score8

  • Many resources use a cuttoff of ≥ 3 as high risk

text.content:
FeatureAnti-GBM DiseaseGranulomatosis with polyangiitis (Wegener)
MechanismAnti-GBM IgG (α-3 type IV collagen)
ANCA-mediated (anti-PR3 / c-ANCA)
SerologyAnti-GBM antibodies
c-ANCA / anti-PR3 positive (~75%)
Renal IFLinear IgG deposition
Pauci-immune (no deposits)
GlomerulonephritisCrescentic GNCrescentic GN
PulmonaryDiffuse alveolar haemorrhage
Nodules, cavities, infiltrates ± haemorrhage
Upper airwayAbsent
Sinusitis, epistaxis, saddle nose, subglottic stenosis
OcularAbsent
Scleritis, episcleritis, proptosis
Renal-only diseasePossible (< 40% cases)Possible
EpidemiologyBimodal: young men + older women
Uniform adult distribution
Relapse riskLow (monophasic)
High (especially PR3-ANCA+)
TreatmentPlasmapheresis + immunosuppression
Immunosuppression (± plasmapheresis if severe)
Double-positiveUp to 30% also ANCA+; more relapses

text.content:
FeatureSerotonin SyndromeNeuroleptic Malignant Syndrome (NMS)
Typical CauseSSRIs, MAOIs, MDMA, or new psychoactive stimulantsAntipsychotic medications
OnsetRapid (usually within hours)Gradual (can take hours to days)
ReflexesOveractive reflexes; muscle twitching or clonusReduced reflexes, 'lead-pipe' rigidity
PupilsOften enlargedUsually normal
Autonomic SymptomsFast heart rate, elevated blood pressure, fever, excessive sweating
Lab Findings↑ CK (more classically in NMS)
Management generallySupportive care: fluids, benzodiazepines
Management severe casesCyproheptadine or chlorpromazineDantrolene

text.content: Alternate re-entry loops: Functional circuit in AVNRT (left), anatomical circuit in AVRT (right)

rich_text.plain_text: Alternate re-entry loops: Functional circuit in AVNRT (left), anatomical circuit in AVRT (right)

text.content:
" src="AV_reentry_circuit.jpg">
Mechanism of re-entry in slow-fast AVNRT:
1) A premature atrial contraction (PAC) arrives while the fast pathway is still refractory, and is directed down the slow pathway
2) The ERP in the fast pathway ends, and the PAC impulse travels retrogradely up the fast pathway
3) The impulse continually cycles around the two pathways

rich_text.plain_text:
" src="AV_reentry_circuit.jpg">
Mechanism of re-entry in slow-fast AVNRT:
1) A premature atrial contraction (PAC) arrives while the fast pathway is still refractory, and is directed down the slow pathway
2) The ERP in the fast pathway ends, and the PAC impulse travels retrogradely up the fast pathway
3) The impulse continually cycles around the two pathways
